title: Spectral criteria for generalization in unsupervised Hebbian nets
authors: Elena Agliari, Paulo Duarte Mourão, Alberto Fachechi, Pierpaolo Vivo
abstract: We consider an unsupervised Hebbian network where the pairwise interactions among neurons are built on noisy realizations of hidden ground-truth vectors. Unlike classical Hopfield models, designed as memory devices, this class of networks can be employed to extract latent structure and generalize beyond the "training" set. By combining random matrix theory and replica methods, we derive the asymptotic spectrum of the corresponding interaction matrix and show that the onset of generalization is controlled by a sharp spectral transition. Depending on the quality and the size of the accessible dataset, the spectrum displays either two separated bulks, encoding informative and noisy directions, or a merged single-bulk phase where such distinction is lost. We show that, when coupled with regularization, the emergence of such a spectral split predicts the network's capability to reconstruct the ground-truth vectors from corrupted samples.
summary: This work develops a theoretical framework using random matrix theory to analyze unsupervised Hebbian networks. It shows that the network's ability to generalize is directly encoded in the spectral structure of its interaction matrix. A clear spectral separation between signal and noise directions is predicted to be the necessary condition for successful reconstruction of underlying patterns.
main_problem: To derive the asymptotic spectral properties of the interaction matrix in unsupervised Hebbian networks and establish spectral criteria that predict the network's ability to generalize beyond training data.
main_result: Generalization capability is predicted by the spectral transition: the existence of two separated spectral bulks (signal/noise separation) indicates successful generalization, while a merged single-bulk phase implies failure.
method: The analysis combines random matrix theory and replica methods to compute the quenched free energy and derive the limiting spectral density of the interaction matrix.
model_or_system: The model is an unsupervised Hebbian network trained on noisy realizations of hidden ground-truth vectors, extending classical Hopfield models from simple memory retrieval to latent structure extraction.
key_observables: The asymptotic spectrum ($	ilde{ho}'(\lambda)$), the spectral gap (separation into two bulks), and the overlap measures (archetype and example overlaps).
important_parameters: Network load ($\alpha$), noise parameter ($r$), regularization parameter ($t$), and the ratio $\alpha M$ which dictates the spectral regime.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the thermodynamic limit ($N 	o \infty$) and the use of the replica-symmetric ansatz for order parameters.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the theoretical spectral predictions versus numerical simulations, showing the spectral evolution under regularization and comparing spectral analysis results with overlap measurements across different load regimes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first deriving the spectral density using random matrix theory and replica methods, then analyzing the effect of regularization ($t$) on this spectrum, and finally establishing the spectral gap as the necessary and sufficient condition for successful generalization.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical framework connecting spectral properties (eigenvalue distribution) to functional performance (generalization) provides a powerful, generalizable diagnostic tool applicable to complex quantum or classical learning systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Spectrum of the unsupervised model characterized by the interaction matrix ˜ J H in Eq. (7), for r = 0.3 (left) and r = 0.5 (right). The histogram in green represents the data resulting from computing the eigenvalues of interaction matrices with N = 1000 neurons across 50 independent samples, while the solid black line shows the corresponding theoretical prediction. The load and number of examples were fixed at α = 0.1 and M = 50, respectively.

Fig. 2: Spectrum of the unsupervised regularized model characterized by the interaction matrix ˜ J D(t) in Eq. (8), for r = 0.3 (left) and r = 0.5 (right), and t = 1 (above) and t = 10 (below). The histogram in green represents the data resulting from computing the eigenvalues of interaction matrices with N = 1000 neurons across 50 independent samples, while the black solid line shows the corresponding theoretical prediction. The load and number of examples were fixed at α = 0.1 and M = 50, respectively.
